1: wĕʾēlleh dibrê-hassēper ʾăšer šālaḥ yirmĕyāhû hannābîʾ — "These are the words of the letter that the prophet Jeremiah sent"; sēper (letter, document, scroll) — a formal written communication carried by official envoys. The recipients are specified in layers: "surviving elders among the exiles" (yeter ziknê haggôlâ), priests, prophets, and all the people. "Surviving elders" (yeter) implies some had already died — the exile was not gentle. The MKT preserves "surviving" to acknowledge the mortality within the exile community.
2: The chronological anchoring: ʾaḥărê ṣēt Yĕkonyāhû hammelek wĕhaggĕbîrâ wĕhassārîsîm śārê Yĕhûdâ wîrûšālaim wĕheḥārāš wĕhammasger — after King Jeconiah, the queen mother, court officials, princes, craftsmen, and metalworkers had been taken to Babylon. The fullness of the leadership vacuum is specified: king, queen mother, officials, princes, skilled workers — all the pillars of a functioning society removed.
3: The letter is entrusted to Ĕlāśāh ben-Šāpān ûGĕmaryāhû ben-Ḥilqiyyāhû — named emissaries of Zedekiah on official diplomatic business with Nebuchadnezzar. Jeremiah uses the official diplomatic channel to send his pastoral letter. The names matter: the Shaphan family were supporters of Jeremiah (cf. Jeremiah 26:24), and Hilkiah was the high priest who found the book of the law in Josiah's day (2 Kings 22:8). The letter travels in trustworthy hands.
4: ʾăšer hĕglêtî mîrûšālaim Bābĕlâ — "whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon"; YHWH's own ownership of the exile — not Nebuchadnezzar's conquest but YHWH's deportation. The pastoral letter begins by reframing the exile's cause: the exiles did not lose to Babylon; YHWH sent them. This reframing is essential to what follows — if YHWH sent them, YHWH can bring them back; if they simply lost a political contest, return is uncertain.
5: bĕnû bāttîm wĕšēbû... ṭĕʿû gannôt wĕʾiklû ʾet-pĕriyāhen — "Build houses and settle in them; plant gardens and eat what they produce"; two paired imperatives: build + settle, plant + eat. The verbs are positive engagement with life in Babylon — not temporary shelter or survival, but establishment. The instruction to plant and harvest implies a multi-year commitment: trees planted do not bear fruit immediately. The MKT's "settle in them" captures the permanence-intention in šēbû (dwell, settle).
6: qĕḥû nāšîm ûhôlîdû bānîm ûbānôt — "Marry and have sons and daughters"; the marriage-and-birth instruction directly reverses what Jeremiah was personally commanded in Jeremiah 16:1-4 (no marriage, because of the coming disaster). Jeremiah personally cannot marry; the exiles should marry and multiply. The instruction "give your daughters to husbands and your sons to wives so that they too may multiply" is a full community-multiplication program in exile. The MKT renders the parallelism of sons/daughters in all its symmetry.
7: wĕdirĕšû ʾet-šĕlôm hāʿîr ʾăšer higlêtî ʾetkem šāmmâ — "Seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you into exile"; šālôm of Babylon is YHWH's goal for the exiles. The same word Hananiah promised Israel (Jeremiah 28:9 — šālôm) is to be the exiles' gift to their captors. "And pray to the LORD on its behalf" (wĕhitpallĕlû bĕʿādāh ʾel-YHWH) — prayer for Babylon's welfare is a covenant obligation. The final clause: kî bišlômāh yihyeh lākem šālôm — "for in its welfare you will have welfare" — the unusual theological claim that Israelite flourishing is tied to Babylonian flourishing. The MKT preserves all three dimensions: seek, pray, because.
8: ʾal-yaššîʾû ʾetkem nĕbîʾêkem ʾăšer-bĕqirbĕkem — "Do not let the prophets and diviners among you deceive you"; the false prophets in Babylon parallel those in Jerusalem (Jeremiah 28:1-17). Kōsĕmîm (diviners) alongside nĕbîʾîm (prophets) — the full range of false intermediaries. "Do not listen to the dreams they are causing you to dream" (ʾal-tišmĕʿû ʾel-ḥalōmôtêkem ʾăšer ʾattem maḥlĕmîm) — the unusual causative ("dreams you are causing to dream") implies that the community's wish-fulfillment desires are generating these dreams.
9: kî bišĕqer hēm nĕbāʾîm lākem bišmî — "For they are prophesying lies to you in my name"; the triple disavowal: "I have not sent them" (lōʾ šĕlaḥtîm). The brevity of the disavowal contrasts with the formal prophetic oracle-form Hananiah deployed (Jeremiah 28:2-4) — YHWH's refutation requires only one phrase.
10: kî kōh ʾāmar YHWH kimlōʾt lĕbābel šibʿîm šānâ — "For thus says the LORD: When seventy years for Babylon are complete"; the seventy-year timeframe (cf. Jeremiah 25:11-12) is given a positive reinterpretation: it is not just a period of punishment but a period with an end. ʾEpqōd ʾetkem (I will come to you, attend to you — the verb pāqad meaning to visit/attend) — the divine attention that has seemed absent will return. "I will fulfill my good promise to bring you back to this place" (dĕbārî haṭṭôb) — the restoration is a kept promise, not an afterthought. The MKT's "good promise" translates haddābār haṭṭôb (the good word/promise).
11: kî ʾānōkî yādaʿtî ʾet-hammaḥăšābôt ʾăšer ʾānōkî ḥōšēb ʿălêkem — "For I know the plans I have for you, declares the LORD"; the famous verse. Maḥăšābôt (thoughts, plans, purposes) — the same word used for YHWH's purposes in judgment (e.g. Jeremiah 18:11: ḥōšēb ʿălêkem rāʿâ). Here the maḥăšābôt are lĕšālôm wĕlōʾ lĕrāʿâ — for welfare (wholeness, peace) and not for harm. Lātēt lākem ʾaḥărît wĕtiqwâ — to give you a future (ʾaḥărît = end/afterward, the word for "future") and a hope (tiqwâ = hope, also the word for a cord/rope — something that holds you). The MKT renders "a future and a hope" because both nouns carry forward momentum: ʾaḥărît is eschatological orientation; tiqwâ is present confidence about it.
12: ûqĕrāʾtem ʾōtî ûhălaktem wĕhitpallaltĕm ʾēlay — "Then you will call on me and come and pray to me, and I will listen to you"; the three verbs: call (qārāʾ), come (hālaḵ), pray (hitpallēl) — all three directing the exiles toward YHWH. The covenant relationship restored through prayer. The verse follows Jeremiah 29:11's plan-announcement: the plans for welfare have prayer as their medium of access.
13: ûbiqaštĕm ʾōtî ûmĕṣāʾtem kî tidĕrĕšûnnî bĕkol-lĕbabkem — "You will seek me and find me, when you search for me with all your heart"; the deuteronomic condition (Deuteronomy 4:29 — "if you search for him with all your heart and with all your soul") now stated as a promise rather than a conditional. The MKT's "when you search for me with all your heart" preserves the shift from "if" to "when" — the conditional has become an assurance. The verb mĕṣāʾtem (you will find) is stated declaratively.
14: wĕnimṣēʾtî lākem — "I will let myself be found by you"; nimṣēʾtî is the niphal reflexive/passive of māṣāʾ (find): YHWH makes himself findable. The restoration that follows is comprehensive: šabtî ʾet-šĕbûtkem (I will restore your fortunes — the double-šûb formula); gather from all nations; bring back to the place from which I sent you into exile. The MKT translates šabtî ʾet-šĕbûtkem as "restore your fortunes" — the idiom implies a full reversal of all that the exile has taken.
15: The transition: "You may be saying, 'The LORD has appointed prophets for us here in Babylon'" — Jeremiah addresses the community's likely response. The existence of prophets in Babylon is not denied; the question is whether those prophets are authentic. The MKT renders this as a quotation from the community rather than a statement of fact, following the likely sense.
16: yēdaʿ kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾel-hammelek hayôšēb ʿal-kissēʾ Dāwîd — "Know that this is what the LORD says concerning the king who sits on David's throne"; the oracle against those who remained in Jerusalem (Jeremiah 29:16-19) contrasts with the restoration promised to the exiles (Jeremiah 29:10-14). Those who stayed behind and claim prophets are still under judgment; those who went into exile hold the covenant promise.
17: hinnĕnî mĕšallaḥ bāhem ʾet-haḥereb wĕʾet-hārāʿāb wĕʾet-haddāber — "Thus says the LORD: I am going to send sword, famine, and pestilence against them"; the three judgment agents. The image that follows is viscerally unfavorable: "I will make them like rotten figs that are inedible they are so bad" (kattĕʾēnîm hašşōʿārôt ʾăšer lōʾ-tēʾāknâ mērōaʿ). The contrast with the "good figs" of Jeremiah 24 is intentional — those taken to Babylon are good figs; those who stayed are rotten.
18: wĕrādaptî ʾaḥărêhem beḥereb bārāʿāb ûbaddāber — "I will pursue them with sword, famine, and pestilence"; the verb rādap (pursue) is relentless — judgment will not stop. "I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth" — lĕzaʿăwâ (horror/trembling), lĕqĕlālâ (curse), lĕšammâ ûlišrēqâ (desolation and hissing). The fourfold shame vocabulary comprehensively captures the social humiliation of covenant curse.
19: yaʿan ʾăšer lōʾ-šāmĕʿû ʾel-dĕbāray — "because they did not obey my words" — the standard covenant-breach formula. "Which I sent to them persistently through my servants the prophets" (šāllōaḥ wĕšāllōaḥ — the infinitive absolute expressing persistent/repeated sending): YHWH's messengers came repeatedly; the refusal was persistent. The MKT renders "persistently" to capture the emphatic repetition of the infinitive absolute.
20: wĕʾattem šimĕʿû dĕbar-YHWH kol-haggôlâ — "So hear the word of the LORD, all you exiles"; the contrast re-established: the exiles who receive the restoration promise (Jeremiah 29:10-14) are now distinguished from those under judgment (Jeremiah 29:16-19). The address kol-haggôlâ (all the exile) is the audience for the warning about false prophets that follows.
21: ʾel-ʾAhāb ben-Qôlāyāhû wĕʾel-Ṣidqiyyāhû ben-Maʿăśēyāhû — specific false prophets in Babylon named and condemned. Unlike Hananiah whose punishment was death within the year, these two are condemned to become a byword: "May the LORD make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire" (qālāhû... bāʾēš). Execution by fire — they are to be publicly destroyed by the very king they were presumably comforting the exiles about.
22: The curse-formula (qĕlālâ) that will be used by Judean exiles: "May the LORD make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire." The false prophets become proverbial — their fate is made into a curse-saying that will outlive them. The MKT preserves qālāhû (roasted) — the stark word for their execution method.
23: yaʿan ʾăšer ʿāśû nĕbālâ bĕYiśrāʾēl — "because they have done a disgraceful thing in Israel"; nĕbālâ (folly, disgraceful act) is the technical term for sexual immorality and its social-covenant violation. The two charges: adultery with neighbors' wives and speaking false words in YHWH's name. The combination of sexual and prophetic unfaithfulness mirrors Hosea's analysis — false prophecy and sexual immorality are paired covenant violations.
24: The Shemaiah episode is introduced: ûlĕŠĕmāʿyāhû hanne-ḥĕlāmî tōʾmar — a new oracle directed against Shemaiah the Nehelamite. The unusual word Nehelamite may be a place name or a word-play on ḥālam (dream) — the "dreamer" prophet. Shemaiah is not in Jerusalem but another exile in Babylon who wrote counter-letters to Jerusalem.
25: yaʿan ʾăšer šālaḥtā sĕpārîm bĕšimkā — "Because you sent letters in your own name" — Shemaiah's unauthorized counter-letters, sent in his own name to Jerusalem's priest Zephaniah, attempted to subvert Jeremiah's authority. Sending letters bĕšimkā (in your own name) rather than in YHWH's name marks the self-authorization. The MKT preserves this as the formal charge: letters sent in Shemaiah's name, not YHWH's.
26: Shemaiah's letter claimed that Zephaniah the priest had been appointed to replace Jehoiada "to be in charge of the house of the LORD" (lihyôt pĕquddôt bêt-YHWH) and to deal with any "madman who plays the prophet" (kol-ʾîš mĕšuggāʿ ûmitnaббēʾ). The word for Jeremiah used by Shemaiah — mĕšuggāʿ (madman, disturbed person) — is an insult. The MKT translates it directly as "madman who plays the prophet" to preserve the contemptuous force.
27: lāmmâ lōʾ gāʿartā bĕYirmĕyāhû hāʿănātôtî — "Why then have you not rebuked Jeremiah of Anathoth?" Shemaiah names Jeremiah with his village origin — "of Anathoth" — perhaps a further diminishment (a provincial prophet). The expected response: Zephaniah should have used his priestly authority to silence Jeremiah. That he did not is what Shemaiah's letter is designed to correct.
28: kî ʿal-kēn šālaḥ ʾēlênû Bābĕlâ — "For he has sent word to us in Babylon, saying: Your exile will be long (ʾārkâ)"; Shemaiah quotes Jeremiah's letter (Jeremiah 29:5-7) — build houses, plant gardens. The instruction to settle in for a long exile is what Shemaiah finds intolerable. The word ʾārkâ (long, extended) is Shemaiah's characterization of Jeremiah's message — and it is accurate. The MKT follows the quoted speech.
29: Zephaniah read the letter aloud to Jeremiah — the priest showed Jeremiah the letter rather than acting on it. The implication: Zephaniah is not an enemy of Jeremiah, even if Shemaiah wanted him to be. The MKT preserves the "aloud" reading because it implies that the letter was read in Jeremiah's hearing as an act of informing rather than condemning him.
30: The standard prophetic formula introduces the divine response to Shemaiah: wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH ʾel-yirmĕyāhû lēʾmōr. The sequence: Shemaiah's unauthorized letter → Zephaniah reads it to Jeremiah → YHWH speaks to Jeremiah about Shemaiah. YHWH responds to private opposition to his prophet with public divine judgment, just as with Hananiah.
31: šĕlaḥ ʾel-kol-haggôlâ lēʾmōr — "Send word to all the exiles"; Jeremiah is to broadcast the judgment against Shemaiah publicly — to all the exiles, not just to Zephaniah or to Shemaiah privately. The reason for the public condemnation: yaʿan ʾăšer nibbā lākem Šĕmāʿyāhû wĕʾānōkî lōʾ-šĕlaḥtîw — "because Shemaiah has prophesied to you and I did not send him." The core criterion: lōʾ šĕlaḥtîw — unsent prophecy is false prophecy.
32: lāken kōh-ʾāmar YHWH hinnĕnî pōqēd ʿal-Šĕmāʿyāhû — "Therefore thus says the LORD: I am going to punish Shemaiah the Nehelamite and his descendants"; three punishments: none of his descendants will survive (lōʾ-yihyeh lô ʾîš yôšēb bĕtôk hāʿām hazzeh), he will not see the good YHWH does for his people, and he will not return from exile. The punishment for opposing the covenant promise: exclusion from the very blessing he attempted to hasten by false prophecy.
